steps.title: Identify the key conflict in the passage
steps.content: The single origin hypothesis says iron metallurgy began in Anatolia around 2200–2000 BCE and spread outward. But excavations in Ôboui and Gbatoro in Central Africa dated iron workshops to 2300–1900 BCE—contemporary with Anatolia. The blank must complete a logical sentence explaining what this new evidence suggests about the hypothesis.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and find the match
steps.content:
Consider every option:
  • "iron production may have originated in Anatolia much earlier than the available evidence currently indicates.": This would reinforce the single origin hypothesis by pushing Anatolia’s date back further, but the Central African dates are already as old as Anatolia’s, so this doesn’t resolve the problem. ✗
  • "iron production technologies were likely transmitted from Anatolia to Central Africa via an alternate route than the one suggested by some proponents of the single origin hypothesis.": This still assumes Anatolian origin and transmission to Africa, which the simultaneous dates make unlikely. ✗
  • "iron production technologies found in Gbabiri likely derived directly from technologies transmitted from Anatolia, but those found in Ôboui and Gbatoro did not.": Splitting the sites this way is unsupported by anything in the passage and still relies on transmission for part of the evidence. ✗
  • "iron production may have developed independently and relatively simultaneously in Anatolia and parts of Central Africa.": If both regions produced iron at the same time, no transmission is needed—independent invention explains why Central African sites are as old as Anatolian ones. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
